title: Sam Kennedy says Red Sox looking to add bat ‘earlier than ever’
author: Kaley Brown
bullets: Red Sox president Sam Kennedy says the team is seeking to add a hitter earlier than usual this season.
The Red Sox have struggled offensively with the departures of Devers and Bregman and injuries to key players.
Boston's offensive stats include a .240 batting average and the second-lowest home run total in MLB with 36.

title: Mock draft predicts Red Sox will select Bishop Feehan star in first round
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Red Sox predicted to draft Brody Bumila, a Bishop Feehan high school left-hander, at 20th in MLB.com's mock draft.
Scouts, including several teams' directors, attended Bumila's recent game where he struck out 14 but allowed four runs in the seventh.
Bumila's fastball noted for speed, lacks developed secondary pitches; some predict a second-round pick despite potential shown.

title: Red Sox’ Trevor Story undergoes sports hernia surgery
author: Kaley Brown
bullets: Red Sox shortstop Trevor Story undergoes successful sports hernia surgery, likely sidelined for 6-10 weeks.
Story's latest surgery follows previous procedures on his shoulder and elbow since joining Red Sox in 2022.
Marcelo Mayer will likely start at shortstop for the first time in the majors on Sunday.

title: Peter Wolf left one Boston art school to form the J. Geils Band. Six decades later, he earned an honorary doctorate from another.
author: Lauren Daley
bullets: Peter Wolf, former frontman of J. Geils Band, was awarded an honorary doctorate by Massachusetts College of Art and Design.
Wolf, originally from the Bronx, attended the Museum School of Fine Arts in Boston before forming his band.
Wolf plans to release a new album and possibly a book following his honorary degree announcement.

title: Boston 25 hires Lauren Walsh for newly-created role
author: Chad Finn
bullets: Lauren Walsh joins Boston 25 as a sports media journalist, a newly-created position at the station.
Walsh, a Medford native and Syracuse graduate, previously worked at WSMV in Nashville as a sports anchor and reporter.
She has also worked in Winston-Salem, N.C., Burlington, Vt., and Syracuse, N.Y., covering local sports teams.

title: Somerville restaurant owner says viral ‘pool water’ reply to customer review was unauthorized
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: Olivia's Kitchen in Somerville faced backlash after a viral unauthorized response to a customer review was posted online.
Owner Simona Sbano clarified the response was not approved, and the responsible employee has been disciplined and let go.
The restaurant will now serve tap water upon request, addressing complaints related to its previous water service policy.

title: With split vote, City Council effort to reject Wu’s budget fails
author: Ross Cristantiello
bullets: Boston City Council split 6-6 on initiative to reject Mayor Wu's budget, revealing divided opinions on fiscal strategies.
Councilors debating Wu's budget cited concerns over cuts and sought leverage in amendment discussions before the June 10 deadline.
Mayor Wu resisted council pressure to boost spending, citing use of $70M reserves to cover deficits caused by unusual snowfall costs.

title: Kevin McHale questions Celtics’ 3-point heavy approach: ‘The ball has got to touch the paint’
author: Khari A. Thompson
bullets: Kevin McHale criticized the Celtics' heavy reliance on 3-point shots under coach Joe Mazzulla's strategy.
The Celtics led the NBA in 3-point attempts but struggled with points in the paint and free-throw attempts.
Brad Stevens acknowledged a need for more shot variance and potential team additions to address scoring issues.

title: Mass. pols issue report on possible effects of Haiti TPS termination on local health care
author: Samantha Genzer
bullets: The report by Sen. Warren, Rep. Pressley, and Sen. Markey warns ending TPS for Haitians would worsen U.S. healthcare shortages.
Approximately 13,000 Haitian TPS holders work as nursing assistants, impacting 65,000 patients daily in Massachusetts.
Ending TPS affects around 45,000 Massachusetts residents, risking job loss and increased healthcare system burdens.

title: This New England town hosts one of America’s best 4th of July celebrations, according to USA Today readers
author: Kristi Palma
bullets: Bristol, R.I.'s Fourth of July Celebration ranked No. 5 in USA Today's 2026 10Best Readers' Choice Awards.
Bristol's celebration, starting in 1785, is the nation's oldest continuous Fourth of July event, involving a three-week festival.
More than 200,000 attendees watch Bristol's Military, Civic, and Firemen’s Parade on July 4 each year.
